title: Work Breakdown Structure definition and primary purpose
overview: Defines the Work Breakdown Structure and explains its primary purpose as a deliverables-based scope artifact.
detailed_summary: A Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) is a hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team to accomplish project objectives and create required deliverables. Its purpose is to represent all deliverables to be produced by the project, not to list activities, costs, durations, dependencies, or resource assignments. The WBS answers the question, "What are we going to produce as a result of the project?" and serves as a higher-level artifact that supports creation of schedules, budgets, and other planning outputs.
content: The WBS is a higher-level project artifact focused on deliverables. It represents all of the deliverables the project will produce and provides the basis for later defining activities, estimating costs, sequencing work, and building schedules and budgets. It is not itself a scheduling or budgeting deliverable. The WBS exists to establish agreement on project scope by documenting what the project will produce. It answers the core question: "What are we going to produce as a result of the project?" The answer is the set of WBS deliverables.
relationships: WBS defines deliverables before activities are created
WBS is a scope artifact that supports schedule and budget creation
search_queries: What is the purpose of a Work Breakdown Structure?
Why do projects use a WBS?
common_questions: What is the primary purpose of the WBS on a project?
Does a WBS show what the project will produce?
Is the WBS a scope document?
can_answer: What information belongs in a WBS
examples: A WBS for a mountain bike project would list deliverables such as bike components rather than tasks to build them.
best_practices: Use the WBS to establish shared understanding of project deliverables.
Treat the WBS as the main artifact for communicating project scope.
warnings: Do not confuse the WBS with a project schedule or budget.
limitations: The WBS does not describe how work is sequenced or timed.
troubleshooting: If stakeholders describe activities, costs, or dates instead of outputs, refocus the discussion on deliverables.
decision_rules: If an item describes what will be produced, it may belong in the WBS.
If an item describes how, when, who, or how much, it belongs in another planning artifact rather than the WBS.
